g.data-narration: Welcome to Permutations! Today we explore ordered arrangements, a fundamental concept in counting and combinatorics for computer science.
A permutation is an ordered arrangement where the sequence matters. Unlike combinations, AB and BA are considered different arrangements.
g.text: An ordered arrangement of r distinct objects selected from n objects.
Each arrangement is unique because order matters!

g.data-narration: The Fundamental Principle of Counting states that if one task has m ways and another has n ways, both can be done in m×n ways.
g.text: If task A can be done in m ways and task B in n ways,
then both can be done in m × n ways.

g.data-narration: The formula P(n,r) equals n factorial divided by n-r factorial. This counts arrangements of r objects chosen from n distinct objects.
g.text: Number of ways to arrange r objects from n distinct objects:

g.data-narration: Two important special cases: when r equals n, we get n factorial (arranging all objects). When r equals 1, we simply have n choices.
Let's solve: In how many ways can 3 students be seated in a row of 5 chairs? Using P(5,3) gives us 60 possible seatings.
g.text: Problem: In how many ways can 3 students be seated in a row of 5 chairs?

g.data-narration: When objects repeat, we divide by factorials of each repeated type. For n objects with n1, n2... repetitions, use n!/(n1!×n2!×...).
g.text: When some objects are identical, divide by their factorials:

g.data-narration: For the word STATISTICS, we have repeated letters: S=3, T=3, I=2. Total arrangements: 10!/(3!×3!×2!) equals 50,400.
g.text: Problem: How many arrangements from letters of "STATISTICS"?
Letters: S-S-S, T-T-T, A, I-I, C (10 letters)

g.data-narration: For circular arrangements, we fix one position and arrange remaining n-1 objects. The formula is (n-1)! because rotations are considered identical.
g.text: Arranging objects in a circle where rotations are identical:

g.data-narration: For 6 friends sitting around a table with two friends together, treat them as one unit: (5-1)! × 2! equals 48 ways.
g.text: Problem: 6 friends around a table, 2 particular friends must sit together

g.data-narration: A derangement is a permutation where no element stays in its original position, like shuffling cards where no card remains in its place.
g.text: Permutations where NO element appears in its original position:

g.data-narration: Common derangement values: D1=0, D2=1, D3=2, D4=9, D5=44, D6=265. These appear frequently in probability problems.
Here are all key permutation formulas in one place: P(n,r), repeated objects, circular, and derangements for your exam preparation.
Remember: order matters in permutations. Don't forget to divide by n in circular arrangements. Watch for repeated objects. Practice previous year questions!
